[[ja:甲府盆地]]

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

revision.text:
'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

[[ja:甲府盆地]]

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.

In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.

The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}

Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}

== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.

The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}

Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}

== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

[[ja:甲府盆地]]

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}

== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

[[ja:甲府盆地]]

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}

The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

[[ja:甲府盆地]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]]

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}

Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient burial mounds such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.
