'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000qq1876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. '''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. '''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}}== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

[[ja:甲府盆地]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:ōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kōfu Basin}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōfu Basin}} [[Category:New]]

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

revision.text:
'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

[[ja:甲府盆地]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:ōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kōfu Basin}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kōfu Basin}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kōfu Basin]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]]

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町}}
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県}}, Japan. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.

In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.

The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン}}. {{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}

The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地|12=draft}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地|12=draft}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地|12=draft}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地|12=draft}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市|12=draft}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候|12=draft}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨|12=draft}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川|12=draft}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川|12=draft}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川|12=draft}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川|12=draft}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹|12=draft}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕|12=draft}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線|12=draft}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道|12=draft}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号|12=draft}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道|12=draft}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線|12=draft}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼|12=draft}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺|12=draft}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑|12=draft}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群|12=draft}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳|12=draft}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|12=draft}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事|12=draft}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ|12=draft}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ|12=draft}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ|12=draft}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン|12=draft}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏|12=draft}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=draft}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=draft}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=draft}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=draft}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町|12=draft}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町|12=draft}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町|12=draft}}
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=draft}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=draft}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川|12=draft}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川|12=draft}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹|12=draft}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕|12=draft}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線|12=draft}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道|12=draft}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号|12=draft}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道|12=draft}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線|12=draft}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼|12=draft}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺|12=draft}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑|12=draft}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群|12=draft}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳|12=draft}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|12=draft}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事|12=draft}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ|12=draft}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ|12=draft}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ|12=draft}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン|12=draft}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏|12=draft}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=draft}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=draft}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=draft}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=draft}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町|12=draft}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町|12=draft}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町|12=draft}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=draft}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=draft}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

'''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地|12=draft}} located in central {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|ja|山梨県|12=draft}}, Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features {{ill|alluvial fans|ja|扇状地|12=draft}}. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other {{ill|tectonic basins|ja|構造盆地|12=draft}} such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地|12=draft}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=draft}} in {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}}. The basin includes the prefectural capital {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft}}, as well as cities such as {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市|12=draft}}.
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候|12=draft}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent {{ill|localized heavy rainfall|ja|集中豪雨|12=draft}}.
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the {{ill|Fuefuki River|ja|笛吹川|12=draft}} from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川|12=draft}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the {{ill|Fuji River|ja|富士川|12=draft}}, which flows toward {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川|12=draft}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹|12=draft}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕|12=draft}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the {{ill|Chuo Main Line|ja|中央本線|12=draft}}, which runs from the northwest to the east, the {{ill|Chuo Expressway|ja|中央自動車道|12=draft}}, and {{ill|National Route 20 (Japan)|ja|国道20号|12=draft}} (formerly the {{ill|Koshu Kaido|ja|甲州街道|12=draft}}), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, {{ill|National Route 52 (Japan)|ja|国道52号|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Minobu Line|ja|身延線|12=draft}} connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼|12=draft}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=draft}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺|12=draft}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲陽軍鑑|12=draft}} recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)|12=draft}} and {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群|12=draft}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the {{ill|Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代|12=draft}}, the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the {{ill|Kai Choshizuka Kofun|ja|甲斐銚子塚古墳|12=draft}}.
In the medieval period, the {{ill|Takeda clan|ja|武田氏|12=draft}} carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事|12=draft}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including {{ill|grapes|ja|ブドウ|12=draft}}, {{ill|peaches|ja|モモ|12=draft}}, and {{ill|cherries|ja|サクランボ|12=draft}}. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for {{ill|wine production|ja|ワイン|12=draft}}. The region's proximity to the {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Area|ja|首都圏 (日本)|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Chukyo Region|ja|中京圏|12=draft}} attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=draft}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsugatake Mountains|ja|八ヶ岳|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=draft}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=draft}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=draft}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * {{ill|Kofu|ja|甲府市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yamanashi|ja|山梨市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Nirasaki|ja|韮崎市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Minami-Alps|ja|南アルプス市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kai|ja|甲斐市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Fuefuki|ja|笛吹市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Koshu|ja|甲州市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Chuo|ja|中央市|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ichikawamisato|ja|市川三郷町|12=draft}} * {{ill|Fujikawa|ja|富士川町|12=draft}} * {{ill|Showa|ja|昭和町|12=draft}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=draft}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=draft}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2024年7月}} [[ファイル:KofuBonchi.jpg|thumbnail|260px|甲府盆地]] [[File:Kofu Basin Relief Map, SRTM-1.jpg|thumbnail|260 px|甲府盆地の地形図]] [[ファイル:Kofu Basin Peach blossom and the Minami Alps.JPG|thumbnail|260 px|桃の花咲く甲府盆地と[[南アルプス]]]] [[ファイル:Kofu-Basin from Mt.Daibosatsurei 01.jpg|thumbnail|260 px|[[大菩薩嶺]]から見た甲府盆地]] '''甲府盆地'''（こうふぼんち、{{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}）は、[[山梨県]]中央部に位置する[[盆地]]。形状は、やや東西に長い逆三角形で、[[扇状地]]になっている。面積は375 [[平方キロメートル|km2]]。
[[長野県]]の[[松本盆地]]や[[諏訪盆地]]と続く[[構造盆地]]。中心部に[[都道府県庁所在地|県庁所在地]]の[[甲府市]]が位置するほか、[[甲州市]]や[[山梨市]]など、多くの市域を含む。 == 地理 == 平均[[標高]]は比較的高い300 mに位置し、[[四季]]が明瞭で寒暖の差が大きい盆地特有の[[内陸性気候]]であり、夏は暑く、冬は寒い。特に夏は日本でも一二を争う高温になる。年間[[降水量]]は少なく1,200 mm程度であるが、山地部への[[降雪]]が[[水資源]]の供給源になっており、夏から秋には[[集中豪雨]]が発生しやすい。北東から流れる[[笛吹川]]と、北西から流れる[[釜無川]]が盆地南西部で合流して[[富士川]]となり、[[静岡県]]へ向かう。盆地西部には[[御勅使川]]や早川などが東流する。河川の堆積作用により700 m以上の砂礫層があり、周縁には御勅使川扇状地、釜無川扇状地、金川扇状地など多くの複合[[扇状地]]が形成され、[[果樹]]栽培や[[養蚕]]に適した地形となっている。中南部の低地はかつて両河川の[[氾濫原]]であり、[[水田]]地帯として利用されてきた。 甲府市を交通の中心に、盆地北西から東には[[中央本線]]が、また、盆地南部を迂回する[[中央自動車道]]と[[国道20号]]（旧[[甲州街道]]）が、盆地西部には南北に[[静岡県]]へ通じる[[国道52号]]と[[身延線]]が通じており、盆地形状が交通の要衝として利用されている。 == 人文的歴史 == 地質学的成因には[[湖沼|地底湖]]説もあり、『[[甲斐国志]]』『[[甲州噺]]』など近世に成立した地誌類には甲府盆地がかつて湖底であったと考える湖水伝説が存在する。近世初頭に成立した『[[甲陽軍鑑]]』に拠れば、太古の甲府盆地は湖であったが法城寺（廃寺）に祀られていた上条地蔵菩薩（国母稲積地蔵）の力によって盆地南部の山が切り開かれ、湖水を富士川に流したという。甲府盆地の湖水伝承を伝える寺社には甲府市の[[穴切大神社]]、甲府市の佐久神社、[[南アルプス市]]の神部神社、[[韮崎市]]の[[穂見神社 (韮崎市旭町)|苗敷山穂見神社]]などがある。 中部地方有数の平野面積を有し、[[旧石器時代 (日本)|旧石器時代]]から[[縄文時代]]前期までは周辺の山岳地帯が主な考古遺跡の分布地域であったが、縄文中期には盆地地域でも[[釈迦堂遺跡群]]など大規模集落が進出し、[[弥生時代]]以降の集落遺跡や水田遺構も見られる。 盆地東南部の[[曽根丘陵]]では弥生時代後期から[[中道往還]]を経て東海地方からの古墳文化が流入し、曽根丘陵では県内最古の古墳である[[小平沢古墳]]から[[ヤマト王権]]の強い影響を受けた最大の[[前方後円墳]]である[[甲斐銚子塚古墳]]や[[岡銚子塚古墳]]が出現し、甲府盆地が有力首長の勢力基盤になっていたと考えられている。[[5世紀]]以降には古墳の築造が盆地各地へ拡散し、盆地北縁では[[渡来人]]集団の生産遺跡が分布し、渡来人の墓制であると考えられている[[積石塚]]が分布している。 古代には盆地西部が中央政府の支配拠点となり、[[国衙]]や古代寺院も出現する。笛吹・釜無の両河川の氾濫原や盆地周縁の山麓地域は[[牧 (古代)|官牧]]として利用され、[[甲斐の黒駒]]に象徴される馬産地となる。[[平安時代]]には[[常陸国]]から移住した[[甲斐源氏]]が盆地各地へ進出し、棟梁となった[[武田氏]]が甲斐守護となり、石和や甲府に居館を置き、甲府盆地は諸勢力との抗争の舞台となる。[[戦国 (日本)|戦国]]期には武田氏の大名権力により治水工事が進められ、[[新田開発]]も行われる。 近世にはさらに[[用水路]]や[[堰]]の開発が進み、扇状地域で普及した[[養蚕]]や果樹栽培など盆地地形を利用した産業が成立する。戦後には産業構造の変化から農業は養蚕や米麦栽培から[[果樹]]栽培へ移行し、[[市街化]]や[[工業団地]]の造成などにより盆地の景観は変貌している。 == 観光 == 甲府盆地に形成された扇状地には、[[ブドウ]]や[[モモ]]、[[サクランボ]]といった多くの果物が栽培されているほか、東部の勝沼周辺では[[ワイン]]の製造も盛んである。これら観光農園やワイナリーも多いことに加え、[[首都圏 (日本)|東京圏]]や[[中京圏]]からも近いことから多くの観光客も訪れる。盆地の北側には多くの奇岩がある渓谷として有名な[[昇仙峡]]があるほか、[[南アルプス]]や[[八ヶ岳]]、[[清里高原]]などへの[[玄関口]]としての役割も担っている。 {{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|[[愛宕山 (甲府市)|愛宕山]]から甲府盆地を望む。左端が東で右端が西。撮影地点の愛宕山は[[奥秩父山塊]]の南端に位置する。[[御坂山地]]越しに[[富士山]]、西側には[[南アルプス]]がそびえ、360度山地に囲まれている。}} == 市町村 == [[甲府市]]・[[山梨市]]・[[韮崎市]]・[[南アルプス市]]・[[甲斐市]]・[[笛吹市]]・[[甲州市]]・[[中央市]]・[[市川三郷町]]・[[富士川町]]・[[昭和町]] == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kofu_Basin}} * [[地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)]] - 甲府盆地底部一帯は日本国内最大の[[日本住血吸虫症]]罹病地帯だったが、[[研究者]]や[[医師]]らによる病気撲滅の結果、[[1996年]]に終息宣言が出されることとなった。1960年代以降、山梨盆地において稲作から果樹栽培への切り替えが急激に進んだのも、地方病対策の一面が大きい。 {{日本の盆地}} {{デフォルトソート:こうふほんち}} [[Category:日本の盆地]] [[Category:扇状地]] [[Category:山梨県の地形]] [[Category:平成百景]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[甲府盆地]] to [[Kōfu Basin]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2024年7月}} [[ファイル:KofuBonchi.jpg|thumbnail|260px|甲府盆地]] [[File:Kofu Basin Relief Map, SRTM-1.jpg|thumbnail|260 px|甲府盆地の地形図]] [[ファイル:Kofu Basin Peach blossom and the Minami Alps.JPG|thumbnail|260 px|桃の花咲く甲府盆地と[[南アルプス]]]] [[ファイル:Kofu-Basin from Mt.Daibosatsurei 01.jpg|thumbnail|260 px|[[大菩薩嶺]]から見た甲府盆地]] '''甲府盆地'''（こうふぼんち、{{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016年1月6日公表|format=PDF}}）は、[[山梨県]]中央部に位置する[[盆地]]。形状は、やや東西に長い逆三角形で、[[扇状地]]になっている。面積は375 [[平方キロメートル|km2]]。
[[長野県]]の[[松本盆地]]や[[諏訪盆地]]と続く[[構造盆地]]。中心部に[[都道府県庁所在地|県庁所在地]]の[[甲府市]]が位置するほか、[[甲州市]]や[[山梨市]]など、多くの市域を含む。 == 地理 == 平均[[標高]]は比較的高い300 mに位置し、[[四季]]が明瞭で寒暖の差が大きい盆地特有の[[内陸性気候]]であり、夏は暑く、冬は寒い。特に夏は日本でも一二を争う高温になる。年間[[降水量]]は少なく1,200 mm程度であるが、山地部への[[降雪]]が[[水資源]]の供給源になっており、夏から秋には[[集中豪雨]]が発生しやすい。北東から流れる[[笛吹川]]と、北西から流れる[[釜無川]]が盆地南西部で合流して[[富士川]]となり、[[静岡県]]へ向かう。盆地西部には[[御勅使川]]や早川などが東流する。河川の堆積作用により700 m以上の砂礫層があり、周縁には御勅使川扇状地、釜無川扇状地、金川扇状地など多くの複合[[扇状地]]が形成され、[[果樹]]栽培や[[養蚕]]に適した地形となっている。中南部の低地はかつて両河川の[[氾濫原]]であり、[[水田]]地帯として利用されてきた。 甲府市を交通の中心に、盆地北西から東には[[中央本線]]が、また、盆地南部を迂回する[[中央自動車道]]と[[国道20号]]（旧[[甲州街道]]）が、盆地西部には南北に[[静岡県]]へ通じる[[国道52号]]と[[身延線]]が通じており、盆地形状が交通の要衝として利用されている。 == 人文的歴史 == 地質学的成因には[[湖沼|地底湖]]説もあり、『[[甲斐国志]]』『[[甲州噺]]』など近世に成立した地誌類には甲府盆地がかつて湖底であったと考える湖水伝説が存在する。近世初頭に成立した『[[甲陽軍鑑]]』に拠れば、太古の甲府盆地は湖であったが法城寺（廃寺）に祀られていた上条地蔵菩薩（国母稲積地蔵）の力によって盆地南部の山が切り開かれ、湖水を富士川に流したという。甲府盆地の湖水伝承を伝える寺社には甲府市の[[穴切大神社]]、甲府市の佐久神社、[[南アルプス市]]の神部神社、[[韮崎市]]の[[穂見神社 (韮崎市旭町)|苗敷山穂見神社]]などがある。 中部地方有数の平野面積を有し、[[旧石器時代 (日本)|旧石器時代]]から[[縄文時代]]前期までは周辺の山岳地帯が主な考古遺跡の分布地域であったが、縄文中期には盆地地域でも[[釈迦堂遺跡群]]など大規模集落が進出し、[[弥生時代]]以降の集落遺跡や水田遺構も見られる。 盆地東南部の[[曽根丘陵]]では弥生時代後期から[[中道往還]]を経て東海地方からの古墳文化が流入し、曽根丘陵では県内最古の古墳である[[小平沢古墳]]から[[ヤマト王権]]の強い影響を受けた最大の[[前方後円墳]]である[[甲斐銚子塚古墳]]や[[岡銚子塚古墳]]が出現し、甲府盆地が有力首長の勢力基盤になっていたと考えられている。[[5世紀]]以降には古墳の築造が盆地各地へ拡散し、盆地北縁では[[渡来人]]集団の生産遺跡が分布し、渡来人の墓制であると考えられている[[積石塚]]が分布している。 古代には盆地西部が中央政府の支配拠点となり、[[国衙]]や古代寺院も出現する。笛吹・釜無の両河川の氾濫原や盆地周縁の山麓地域は[[牧 (古代)|官牧]]として利用され、[[甲斐の黒駒]]に象徴される馬産地となる。[[平安時代]]には[[常陸国]]から移住した[[甲斐源氏]]が盆地各地へ進出し、棟梁となった[[武田氏]]が甲斐守護となり、石和や甲府に居館を置き、甲府盆地は諸勢力との抗争の舞台となる。[[戦国 (日本)|戦国]]期には武田氏の大名権力により治水工事が進められ、[[新田開発]]も行われる。 近世にはさらに[[用水路]]や[[堰]]の開発が進み、扇状地域で普及した[[養蚕]]や果樹栽培など盆地地形を利用した産業が成立する。戦後には産業構造の変化から農業は養蚕や米麦栽培から[[果樹]]栽培へ移行し、[[市街化]]や[[工業団地]]の造成などにより盆地の景観は変貌している。 == 観光 == 甲府盆地に形成された扇状地には、[[ブドウ]]や[[モモ]]、[[サクランボ]]といった多くの果物が栽培されているほか、東部の勝沼周辺では[[ワイン]]の製造も盛んである。これら観光農園やワイナリーも多いことに加え、[[首都圏 (日本)|東京圏]]や[[中京圏]]からも近いことから多くの観光客も訪れる。盆地の北側には多くの奇岩がある渓谷として有名な[[昇仙峡]]があるほか、[[南アルプス]]や[[八ヶ岳]]、[[清里高原]]などへの[[玄関口]]としての役割も担っている。 {{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|[[愛宕山 (甲府市)|愛宕山]]から甲府盆地を望む。左端が東で右端が西。撮影地点の愛宕山は[[奥秩父山塊]]の南端に位置する。[[御坂山地]]越しに[[富士山]]、西側には[[南アルプス]]がそびえ、360度山地に囲まれている。}} == 市町村 == [[甲府市]]・[[山梨市]]・[[韮崎市]]・[[南アルプス市]]・[[甲斐市]]・[[笛吹市]]・[[甲州市]]・[[中央市]]・[[市川三郷町]]・[[富士川町]]・[[昭和町]] == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kofu_Basin}} * [[地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)]] - 甲府盆地底部一帯は日本国内最大の[[日本住血吸虫症]]罹病地帯だったが、[[研究者]]や[[医師]]らによる病気撲滅の結果、[[1996年]]に終息宣言が出されることとなった。1960年代以降、山梨盆地において稲作から果樹栽培への切り替えが急激に進んだのも、地方病対策の一面が大きい。 {{日本の盆地}} {{デフォルトソート:こうふほんち}}
