The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for [[:en:Wine|wine production]]. The region's proximity to the [[:en:Greater Tokyo Area|Tokyo Metropolitan Area]] and the [[:en:Chūkyō metropolitan area|Chukyo Region]] attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosenkyo Gorge|1=Shōsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9336171}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈|1=Akaishi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415350}}, [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Yatsugatake Mountains]], and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosato Plateau|1=Kiyosato Plateau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561414}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Atago (Kofu)|1=Mount Atago (Kofu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11493834}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaka Mountains|1=Misaka Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488873}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]] * [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]] * [[:en:Nirasaki, Yamanashi|Nirasaki]] * [[:en:Minami-Alps, Yamanashi|Minami-Alps]] * [[:en:Kai, Yamanashi|Kai]] * [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki]] * [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] * [[:en:Chūō, Yamanashi|Chuo]] * [[:en:Ichikawamisato|Ichikawamisato]] * [[:en:Fujikawa, Yamanashi|Fujikawa]] * [[:en:Shōwa, Yamanashi|Showa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|1=local disease|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11086612}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/schistosomiasis|1=schistosomiasis japonica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56351164}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kofu Basin]][[fa:حوضه کوفو]][[ja:甲府盆地]][[uk:Западина Кофу]][[zh:甲府盆地]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11577819]] Wikidata name: Kōfu Basin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=basin|WD=Q813672}} located in central [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features [[:en:Alluvial fan|alluvial fans]]. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other [[:en:Structural basin|tectonic basins]] such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumoto Basin|1=Matsumoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531206}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Basin|1=Suwa Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631910}} in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The basin includes the prefectural capital [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]], as well as cities such as [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] and [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]].
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/inland regions|1=inland regions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394719}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent [[:en:Cloudburst|localized heavy rainfall]].
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the [[:en:Fuefuki River|Fuefuki River]] from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamanashi River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士川|1=Fuji River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q596110}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]], which flows toward [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuboshi River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/orchard farming|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 果物|1=fruit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3314483}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sericulture|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養蚕業|1=sericulture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864650}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the [[:en:Chūō Main Line|Chuo Main Line]], which runs from the northwest to the east, the [[:en:Chūō Expressway|Chuo Expressway]], and [[:en:Japan National Route 20|National Route 20 (Japan)]] (formerly the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]]), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, [[:en:Japan National Route 52|National Route 52 (Japan)]] and the [[:en:Minobu Line|Minobu Line]] connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/paleo-lake|1=still waters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q337567}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Kokushi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshu Banashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The [[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kai Kokushi]] recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Paleolithic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本列島の旧石器時代|1=Japanese Paleolithic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q844172}} and [[:en:Jōmon period|Jomon period]]. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakado Site|1=Shakadō Sites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644535}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Choshizuka Kofun]].
In the medieval period, the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]] carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/irrigation|1=irrigation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554415}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including [[:en:Grape|grapes]], [[:en:Peach|peaches]], and [[:en:Cherry|cherries]]. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for [[:en:Wine|wine production]]. The region's proximity to the [[:en:Greater Tokyo Area|Tokyo Metropolitan Area]] and the [[:en:Chūkyō metropolitan area|Chukyo Region]] attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosenkyo Gorge|1=Shōsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9336171}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈|1=Akaishi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415350}}, [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Yatsugatake Mountains]], and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosato Plateau|1=Kiyosato Plateau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561414}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Atago (Kofu)|1=Mount Atago (Kofu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11493834}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaka Mountains|1=Misaka Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488873}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]] * [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]] * [[:en:Nirasaki, Yamanashi|Nirasaki]] * [[:en:Minami-Alps, Yamanashi|Minami-Alps]] * [[:en:Kai, Yamanashi|Kai]] * [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki]] * [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] * [[:en:Chūō, Yamanashi|Chuo]] * [[:en:Ichikawamisato|Ichikawamisato]] * [[:en:Fujikawa, Yamanashi|Fujikawa]] * [[:en:Shōwa, Yamanashi|Showa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|1=local disease|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11086612}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/schistosomiasis|1=schistosomiasis japonica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56351164}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kofu Basin]][[fa:حوضه کوفو]][[ja:甲府盆地]][[uk:Западина Кофу]][[zh:甲府盆地]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11577819]] Wikidata name: Kōfu Basin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=basin|WD=Q813672}} located in central [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features [[:en:Alluvial fan|alluvial fans]]. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other [[:en:Structural basin|tectonic basins]] such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumoto Basin|1=Matsumoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531206}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Basin|1=Suwa Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631910}} in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The basin includes the prefectural capital [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]], as well as cities such as [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] and [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]].
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/inland regions|1=inland regions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394719}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent [[:en:Cloudburst|localized heavy rainfall]].
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the [[:en:Fuefuki River|Fuefuki River]] from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamanashi River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士川|1=Fuji River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q596110}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]], which flows toward [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuboshi River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/orchard farming|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 果物|1=fruit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3314483}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sericulture|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養蚕業|1=sericulture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864650}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the [[:en:Chūō Main Line|Chuo Main Line]], which runs from the northwest to the east, the [[:en:Chūō Expressway|Chuo Expressway]], and [[:en:Japan National Route 20|National Route 20 (Japan)]] (formerly the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]]), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, [[:en:Japan National Route 52|National Route 52 (Japan)]] and the [[:en:Minobu Line|Minobu Line]] connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/paleo-lake|1=still waters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q337567}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Kokushi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshu Banashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The [[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kai Kokushi]] recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Paleolithic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本列島の旧石器時代|1=Japanese Paleolithic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q844172}} and [[:en:Jōmon period|Jomon period]]. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakado Site|1=Shakadō Sites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644535}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Choshizuka Kofun]].
In the medieval period, the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]] carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/irrigation|1=irrigation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554415}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including [[:en:Grape|grapes]], [[:en:Peach|peaches]], and [[:en:Cherry|cherries]]. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for [[:en:Wine|wine production]]. The region's proximity to the [[:en:Greater Tokyo Area|Tokyo Metropolitan Area]] and the [[:en:Chūkyō metropolitan area|Chukyo Region]] attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosenkyo Gorge|1=Shōsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9336171}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈|1=Akaishi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415350}}, [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Yatsugatake Mountains]], and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosato Plateau|1=Kiyosato Plateau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561414}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Atago (Kofu)|1=Mount Atago (Kofu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11493834}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaka Mountains|1=Misaka Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488873}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]] * [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]] * [[:en:Nirasaki, Yamanashi|Nirasaki]] * [[:en:Minami-Alps, Yamanashi|Minami-Alps]] * [[:en:Kai, Yamanashi|Kai]] * [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki]] * [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] * [[:en:Chūō, Yamanashi|Chuo]] * [[:en:Ichikawamisato|Ichikawamisato]] * [[:en:Fujikawa, Yamanashi|Fujikawa]] * [[:en:Shōwa, Yamanashi|Showa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|1=local disease|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11086612}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/schistosomiasis|1=schistosomiasis japonica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56351164}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuboshi River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} and Hayakawa. Transport infrastructure includes the [[:en:Chūō Main Line|Chuo Main Line]], which runs from the northwest to the east, the [[:en:Chūō Expressway|Chuo Expressway]], and [[:en:Japan National Route 20|National Route 20 (Japan)]] (formerly the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]]), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, [[:en:Japan National Route 52|National Route 52 (Japan)]] and the [[:en:Minobu Line|Minobu Line]] connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/paleo-lake|1=still waters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q337567}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Kokushi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshu Banashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The [[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kai Kokushi]] recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Paleolithic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本列島の旧石器時代|1=Japanese Paleolithic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q844172}} and [[:en:Jōmon period|Jomon period]]. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakado Site|1=Shakadō Sites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644535}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Choshizuka Kofun]].
In the medieval period, the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]] carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/irrigation|1=irrigation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554415}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including [[:en:Grape|grapes]], [[:en:Peach|peaches]], and [[:en:Cherry|cherries]]. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for [[:en:Wine|wine production]]. The region's proximity to the [[:en:Greater Tokyo Area|Tokyo Metropolitan Area]] and the [[:en:Chūkyō metropolitan area|Chukyo Region]] attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosenkyo Gorge|1=Shōsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9336171}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈|1=Akaishi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415350}}, [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Yatsugatake Mountains]], and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosato Plateau|1=Kiyosato Plateau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561414}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Atago (Kofu)|1=Mount Atago (Kofu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11493834}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaka Mountains|1=Misaka Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488873}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]] * [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]] * [[:en:Nirasaki, Yamanashi|Nirasaki]] * [[:en:Minami-Alps, Yamanashi|Minami-Alps]] * [[:en:Kai, Yamanashi|Kai]] * [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki]] * [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] * [[:en:Chūō, Yamanashi|Chuo]] * [[:en:Ichikawamisato|Ichikawamisato]] * [[:en:Fujikawa, Yamanashi|Fujikawa]] * [[:en:Shōwa, Yamanashi|Showa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|1=local disease|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11086612}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/schistosomiasis|1=schistosomiasis japonica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56351164}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

<!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--> [[Category:Landforms of Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Heisei 100 Views]] [[Category:Alluvial fans]] [[Category:Drainage basins of Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kofu Basin}} [[uk:Западина Кофу]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kofu Basin}} [[uk:Западина Кофу]] {{wikidata link|Q11577819}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kofu Basin]][[fa:حوضه کوفو]][[ja:甲府盆地]][[uk:Западина Кофу]][[zh:甲府盆地]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11577819]] Wikidata name: Kōfu Basin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=basin|WD=Q813672}} located in central [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features [[:en:Alluvial fan|alluvial fans]]. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other [[:en:Structural basin|tectonic basins]] such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumoto Basin|1=Matsumoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531206}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Basin|1=Suwa Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631910}} in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The basin includes the prefectural capital [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]], as well as cities such as [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] and [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]].
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/inland regions|1=inland regions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394719}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent [[:en:Cloudburst|localized heavy rainfall]].
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the [[:en:Fuefuki River|Fuefuki River]] from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamanashi River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士川|1=Fuji River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q596110}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]], which flows toward [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuboshi River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/orchard farming|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 果物|1=fruit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3314483}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sericulture|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養蚕業|1=sericulture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864650}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the [[:en:Chūō Main Line|Chuo Main Line]], which runs from the northwest to the east, the [[:en:Chūō Expressway|Chuo Expressway]], and [[:en:Japan National Route 20|National Route 20 (Japan)]] (formerly the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]]), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, [[:en:Japan National Route 52|National Route 52 (Japan)]] and the [[:en:Minobu Line|Minobu Line]] connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/paleo-lake|1=still waters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q337567}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Kokushi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshu Banashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The [[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kai Kokushi]] recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Paleolithic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本列島の旧石器時代|1=Japanese Paleolithic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q844172}} and [[:en:Jōmon period|Jomon period]]. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakado Site|1=Shakadō Sites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644535}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Choshizuka Kofun]].
In the medieval period, the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]] carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/irrigation|1=irrigation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554415}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including [[:en:Grape|grapes]], [[:en:Peach|peaches]], and [[:en:Cherry|cherries]]. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for [[:en:Wine|wine production]]. The region's proximity to the [[:en:Greater Tokyo Area|Tokyo Metropolitan Area]] and the [[:en:Chūkyō metropolitan area|Chukyo Region]] attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosenkyo Gorge|1=Shōsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9336171}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈|1=Akaishi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415350}}, [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Yatsugatake Mountains]], and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosato Plateau|1=Kiyosato Plateau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561414}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Atago (Kofu)|1=Mount Atago (Kofu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11493834}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaka Mountains|1=Misaka Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488873}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]] * [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]] * [[:en:Nirasaki, Yamanashi|Nirasaki]] * [[:en:Minami-Alps, Yamanashi|Minami-Alps]] * [[:en:Kai, Yamanashi|Kai]] * [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki]] * [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] * [[:en:Chūō, Yamanashi|Chuo]] * [[:en:Ichikawamisato|Ichikawamisato]] * [[:en:Fujikawa, Yamanashi|Fujikawa]] * [[:en:Shōwa, Yamanashi|Showa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|1=local disease|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11086612}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/schistosomiasis|1=schistosomiasis japonica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56351164}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for [[:en:Wine|wine production]]. The region's proximity to the [[:en:Greater Tokyo Area|Tokyo Metropolitan Area]] and the [[:en:Chūkyō metropolitan area|Chukyo Region]] attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosenkyo Gorge|1=Shōsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9336171}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈|1=Akaishi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415350}}, [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Yatsugatake Mountains]], and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosato Plateau|1=Kiyosato Plateau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561414}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Atago (Kofu)|1=Mount Atago (Kofu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11493834}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaka Mountains|1=Misaka Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488873}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]] * [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]] * [[:en:Nirasaki, Yamanashi|Nirasaki]] * [[:en:Minami-Alps, Yamanashi|Minami-Alps]] * [[:en:Kai, Yamanashi|Kai]] * [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki]] * [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] * [[:en:Chūō, Yamanashi|Chuo]] * [[:en:Ichikawamisato|Ichikawamisato]] * [[:en:Fujikawa, Yamanashi|Fujikawa]] * [[:en:Shōwa, Yamanashi|Showa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|1=local disease|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11086612}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/schistosomiasis|1=schistosomiasis japonica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56351164}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

<!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--><!--Kōfu Basin--> [[Category:Landforms of Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Heisei 100 Views]] [[Category:Alluvial fans]] [[Category:Drainage basins of Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kofu Basin}} [[uk:Западина Кофу]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kofu Basin}} [[uk:Западина Кофу]] {{wikidata link|Q11577819}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kofu Basin]][[fa:حوضه کوفو]][[ja:甲府盆地]][[uk:Западина Кофу]][[zh:甲府盆地]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11577819]] Wikidata name: Kōfu Basin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=basin|WD=Q813672}} located in central [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features [[:en:Alluvial fan|alluvial fans]]. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other [[:en:Structural basin|tectonic basins]] such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumoto Basin|1=Matsumoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531206}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Basin|1=Suwa Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631910}} in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The basin includes the prefectural capital [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]], as well as cities such as [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] and [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]].
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/inland regions|1=inland regions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394719}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent [[:en:Cloudburst|localized heavy rainfall]].
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the [[:en:Fuefuki River|Fuefuki River]] from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamanashi River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士川|1=Fuji River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q596110}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]], which flows toward [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuboshi River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/orchard farming|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 果物|1=fruit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3314483}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sericulture|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養蚕業|1=sericulture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864650}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the [[:en:Chūō Main Line|Chuo Main Line]], which runs from the northwest to the east, the [[:en:Chūō Expressway|Chuo Expressway]], and [[:en:Japan National Route 20|National Route 20 (Japan)]] (formerly the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]]), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, [[:en:Japan National Route 52|National Route 52 (Japan)]] and the [[:en:Minobu Line|Minobu Line]] connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/paleo-lake|1=still waters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q337567}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Kokushi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshu Banashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The [[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kai Kokushi]] recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Paleolithic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本列島の旧石器時代|1=Japanese Paleolithic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q844172}} and [[:en:Jōmon period|Jomon period]]. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakado Site|1=Shakadō Sites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644535}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Choshizuka Kofun]].
In the medieval period, the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]] carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/irrigation|1=irrigation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554415}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including [[:en:Grape|grapes]], [[:en:Peach|peaches]], and [[:en:Cherry|cherries]]. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for [[:en:Wine|wine production]]. The region's proximity to the [[:en:Greater Tokyo Area|Tokyo Metropolitan Area]] and the [[:en:Chūkyō metropolitan area|Chukyo Region]] attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosenkyo Gorge|1=Shōsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9336171}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈|1=Akaishi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415350}}, [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Yatsugatake Mountains]], and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosato Plateau|1=Kiyosato Plateau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561414}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Atago (Kofu)|1=Mount Atago (Kofu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11493834}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaka Mountains|1=Misaka Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488873}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]] * [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]] * [[:en:Nirasaki, Yamanashi|Nirasaki]] * [[:en:Minami-Alps, Yamanashi|Minami-Alps]] * [[:en:Kai, Yamanashi|Kai]] * [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki]] * [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] * [[:en:Chūō, Yamanashi|Chuo]] * [[:en:Ichikawamisato|Ichikawamisato]] * [[:en:Fujikawa, Yamanashi|Fujikawa]] * [[:en:Shōwa, Yamanashi|Showa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|1=local disease|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11086612}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/schistosomiasis|1=schistosomiasis japonica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56351164}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kofu Basin]][[fa:حوضه کوفو]][[ja:甲府盆地]][[uk:Западина Кофу]][[zh:甲府盆地]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kofu Basin]][[fa:حوضه کوفو]][[ja:甲府盆地]][[uk:Западина Кофу]][[zh:甲府盆地]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11577819]] Wikidata name: Kōfu Basin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=basin|WD=Q813672}} located in central [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features [[:en:Alluvial fan|alluvial fans]]. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other [[:en:Structural basin|tectonic basins]] such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumoto Basin|1=Matsumoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531206}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Basin|1=Suwa Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631910}} in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The basin includes the prefectural capital [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]], as well as cities such as [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] and [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]].
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/inland regions|1=inland regions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394719}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent [[:en:Cloudburst|localized heavy rainfall]].
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the [[:en:Fuefuki River|Fuefuki River]] from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamanashi River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士川|1=Fuji River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q596110}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]], which flows toward [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuboshi River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/orchard farming|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 果物|1=fruit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3314483}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sericulture|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養蚕業|1=sericulture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864650}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the [[:en:Chūō Main Line|Chuo Main Line]], which runs from the northwest to the east, the [[:en:Chūō Expressway|Chuo Expressway]], and [[:en:Japan National Route 20|National Route 20 (Japan)]] (formerly the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]]), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, [[:en:Japan National Route 52|National Route 52 (Japan)]] and the [[:en:Minobu Line|Minobu Line]] connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/paleo-lake|1=still waters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q337567}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Kokushi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshu Banashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The [[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kai Kokushi]] recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Paleolithic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本列島の旧石器時代|1=Japanese Paleolithic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q844172}} and [[:en:Jōmon period|Jomon period]]. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakado Site|1=Shakadō Sites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644535}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Choshizuka Kofun]].
In the medieval period, the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]] carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/irrigation|1=irrigation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554415}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including [[:en:Grape|grapes]], [[:en:Peach|peaches]], and [[:en:Cherry|cherries]]. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for [[:en:Wine|wine production]]. The region's proximity to the [[:en:Greater Tokyo Area|Tokyo Metropolitan Area]] and the [[:en:Chūkyō metropolitan area|Chukyo Region]] attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosenkyo Gorge|1=Shōsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9336171}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈|1=Akaishi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415350}}, [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Yatsugatake Mountains]], and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosato Plateau|1=Kiyosato Plateau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561414}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Atago (Kofu)|1=Mount Atago (Kofu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11493834}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaka Mountains|1=Misaka Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488873}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]] * [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]] * [[:en:Nirasaki, Yamanashi|Nirasaki]] * [[:en:Minami-Alps, Yamanashi|Minami-Alps]] * [[:en:Kai, Yamanashi|Kai]] * [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki]] * [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] * [[:en:Chūō, Yamanashi|Chuo]] * [[:en:Ichikawamisato|Ichikawamisato]] * [[:en:Fujikawa, Yamanashi|Fujikawa]] * [[:en:Shōwa, Yamanashi|Showa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|1=local disease|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11086612}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/schistosomiasis|1=schistosomiasis japonica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56351164}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Atago (Kofu)|1=Mount Atago (Kofu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11493834}}. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent [[:en:Cloudburst|localized heavy rainfall]].
Two main rivers flow through the basin: the [[:en:Fuefuki River|Fuefuki River]] from the northeast and the {{ill|Kamanashi River|ja|釜無川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamanashi River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 富士川|1=Fuji River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q596110}} from the northwest. They converge in the southwest to form the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]], which flows toward [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuboshi River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/orchard farming|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 果物|1=fruit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3314483}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sericulture|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養蚕業|1=sericulture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864650}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the [[:en:Chūō Main Line|Chuo Main Line]], which runs from the northwest to the east, the [[:en:Chūō Expressway|Chuo Expressway]], and [[:en:Japan National Route 20|National Route 20 (Japan)]] (formerly the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]]), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, [[:en:Japan National Route 52|National Route 52 (Japan)]] and the [[:en:Minobu Line|Minobu Line]] connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/paleo-lake|1=still waters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q337567}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Kokushi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshu Banashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The [[:en:Kōyō Gunkan|Kai Kokushi]] recounts a myth where the waters of the basin were drained by a deity, leaving fertile land for human settlement.
The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Paleolithic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本列島の旧石器時代|1=Japanese Paleolithic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q844172}} and [[:en:Jōmon period|Jomon period]]. Early large settlements, such as the {{ill|Shakado Site|ja|釈迦堂遺跡群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shakado Site|1=Shakadō Sites|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11644535}}, emerged in the Jomon middle period. During the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Choshizuka Kofun]].
In the medieval period, the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]] carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/irrigation|1=irrigation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554415}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including [[:en:Grape|grapes]], [[:en:Peach|peaches]], and [[:en:Cherry|cherries]]. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for [[:en:Wine|wine production]]. The region's proximity to the [[:en:Greater Tokyo Area|Tokyo Metropolitan Area]] and the [[:en:Chūkyō metropolitan area|Chukyo Region]] attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosenkyo Gorge|1=Shōsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9336171}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈|1=Akaishi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415350}}, [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Yatsugatake Mountains]], and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosato Plateau|1=Kiyosato Plateau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561414}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Atago (Kofu)|1=Mount Atago (Kofu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11493834}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaka Mountains|1=Misaka Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488873}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]] * [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]] * [[:en:Nirasaki, Yamanashi|Nirasaki]] * [[:en:Minami-Alps, Yamanashi|Minami-Alps]] * [[:en:Kai, Yamanashi|Kai]] * [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki]] * [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] * [[:en:Chūō, Yamanashi|Chuo]] * [[:en:Ichikawamisato|Ichikawamisato]] * [[:en:Fujikawa, Yamanashi|Fujikawa]] * [[:en:Shōwa, Yamanashi|Showa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|1=local disease|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11086612}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/schistosomiasis|1=schistosomiasis japonica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56351164}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kofu Basin]][[fa:حوضه کوفو]][[ja:甲府盆地]][[uk:Западина Кофу]][[zh:甲府盆地]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kofu Basin]][[fa:حوضه کوفو]][[ja:甲府盆地]][[uk:Западина Кофу]][[zh:甲府盆地]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kofu Basin]][[fa:حوضه کوفو]][[ja:甲府盆地]][[uk:Западина Кофу]][[zh:甲府盆地]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11577819]] Wikidata name: Kōfu Basin [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:48, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kofu Basin''' (甲府盆地, {{lang-en-short|Kofu Basin}}<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000111876.pdf#page=13|title=地名の英語表記及び外国人にわかりやすい地図記号について|author=外国人にわかりやすい地図表現検討会|page=13|publisher=国土地理院|date=2016th year 1月6日公表|format=PDF}}</ref>) is a {{ill|basin|ja|盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=basin|WD=Q813672}} located in central [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]], Japan. Its shape resembles an inverted triangle elongated east-west and features [[:en:Alluvial fan|alluvial fans]]. The basin spans an area of 375 square kilometers and connects with other [[:en:Structural basin|tectonic basins]] such as the {{ill|Matsumoto Basin|ja|松本盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsumoto Basin|1=Matsumoto Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11531206}} and {{ill|Suwa Basin|ja|諏訪盆地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suwa Basin|1=Suwa Basin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11631910}} in [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]. The basin includes the prefectural capital [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]], as well as cities such as [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] and [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]].
== Geography == The basin has an average altitude of 300 meters, featuring a climate typical of {{ill|inland regions|ja|内陸性気候|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/inland regions|1=inland regions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394719}} with distinct seasons and significant temperature fluctuations. Summers are particularly hot, with some of the highest temperatures in Japan, while winters are cold. The annual precipitation is around 1,200 mm, with mountain snowfall providing essential water resources. Summer and autumn see frequent [[:en:Cloudburst|localized heavy rainfall]].

They converge in the southwest to form the [[:en:Fuji River|Fuji River]], which flows toward [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]]. The western part of the basin is fed by tributaries such as the {{ill|Mitsuboshi River|ja|御勅使川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuboshi River|1=Midai River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488828}} and Hayakawa. Extensive alluvial fan formations, such as the Mitsuboshi and Kamanashi fans, have created fertile soil ideal for {{ill|orchard farming|ja|果樹|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/orchard farming|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 果物|1=fruit|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3314483}} and {{ill|sericulture|ja|養蚕|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sericulture|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養蚕業|1=sericulture|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q864650}}, while the lowlands in the southern-central area were historically used for rice paddies.
Transport infrastructure includes the [[:en:Chūō Main Line|Chuo Main Line]], which runs from the northwest to the east, the [[:en:Chūō Expressway|Chuo Expressway]], and [[:en:Japan National Route 20|National Route 20 (Japan)]] (formerly the [[:en:Kōshū Kaidō|Koshu Kaido]]), which bypasses the basin's southern edge. Additionally, [[:en:Japan National Route 52|National Route 52 (Japan)]] and the [[:en:Minobu Line|Minobu Line]] connect the basin's western side to Shizuoka Prefecture.
== Historical Significance == Geological studies suggest that the Kofu Basin might have originated as a {{ill|paleo-lake|ja|湖沼|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/paleo-lake|1=still waters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q337567}}. Legends from Edo-period texts such as the {{ill|Kai Kokushi|ja|甲斐国志|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kai Kokushi|1=Kaikokushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11577871}} and {{ill|Koshu Banashi|ja|甲州噺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Koshu Banashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} describe the basin as once being submerged under a lake. The basin has hosted human activity since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|ja|旧石器時代 (日本)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Paleolithic|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 日本列島の旧石器時代|1=Japanese Paleolithic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q844172}} and [[:en:Jōmon period|Jomon period]]. During the [[:en:Yayoi period|Yayoi period]], the Sone Hills in the southeastern part of the basin became a focal point for cultural exchange with the Tokai region, evidenced by ancient Kofuns such as the [[:en:Kai Chōshizuka Kofun|Kai Choshizuka Kofun]].
In the medieval period, the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]] carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/irrigation|1=irrigation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554415}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including [[:en:Grape|grapes]], [[:en:Peach|peaches]], and [[:en:Cherry|cherries]]. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for [[:en:Wine|wine production]]. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosenkyo Gorge|1=Shōsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9336171}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈|1=Akaishi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415350}}, [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Yatsugatake Mountains]], and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosato Plateau|1=Kiyosato Plateau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561414}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.

In the medieval period, the [[:en:Takeda clan|Takeda clan]] carried out large-scale land reclamation and {{ill|irrigation|ja|治水工事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/irrigation|1=irrigation|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11554415}}, transforming the basin into an agricultural hub. In the Edo period, the development of irrigation canals and levees facilitated the rise of sericulture and fruit cultivation, which continue to shape the region's economy and landscape today.
== Tourism == The basin is renowned for its agricultural products, including [[:en:Grape|grapes]], [[:en:Peach|peaches]], and [[:en:Cherry|cherries]]. The eastern area around Katsunuma is famous for [[:en:Wine|wine production]]. The region's proximity to the [[:en:Greater Tokyo Area|Tokyo Metropolitan Area]] and the [[:en:Chūkyō metropolitan area|Chukyo Region]] attracts many visitors to its farms and wineries. Other notable attractions include the {{ill|Shosenkyo Gorge|ja|昇仙峡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shosenkyo Gorge|1=Shōsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q9336171}}, known for its striking rock formations, as well as the nearby {{ill|Southern Alps|ja|南アルプス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Alps|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 赤石山脈|1=Akaishi Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q415350}}, [[:en:Southern Yatsugatake Volcanic Group|Yatsugatake Mountains]], and {{ill|Kiyosato Plateau|ja|清里高原|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyosato Plateau|1=Kiyosato Plateau|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11561414}}, which serve as gateways for outdoor activities.
{{Wide image|Kofu Basin panorama from Mt Atago Nov. 2013.jpg|1700px|View of the Kofu Basin from {{ill|Mount Atago (Kofu)|ja|愛宕山 (甲府市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Atago (Kofu)|1=Mount Atago (Kofu)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11493834}}. The eastern edge is on the left, and the western edge is on the right. The basin is surrounded by the {{ill|Misaka Mountains|ja|御坂山地|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misaka Mountains|1=Misaka Mountains|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488873}}, Mount Fuji to the south, and the Southern Alps to the west.}}
== Municipalities == The Kofu Basin includes the following municipalities: * [[:en:Kōfu|Kofu]] * [[:en:Yamanashi (city)|Yamanashi]] * [[:en:Nirasaki, Yamanashi|Nirasaki]] * [[:en:Minami-Alps, Yamanashi|Minami-Alps]] * [[:en:Kai, Yamanashi|Kai]] * [[:en:Fuefuki|Fuefuki]] * [[:en:Kōshū, Yamanashi|Koshu]] * [[:en:Chūō, Yamanashi|Chuo]] * [[:en:Ichikawamisato|Ichikawamisato]] * [[:en:Fujikawa, Yamanashi|Fujikawa]] * [[:en:Shōwa, Yamanashi|Showa]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|ja|地方病 (日本住血吸虫症)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Endemic disease (Japanese schistosomiasis)|1=local disease|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11086612}}: The Kofu Basin was the largest region in Japan affected by {{ill|schistosomiasis|ja|日本住血吸虫症|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/schistosomiasis|1=schistosomiasis japonica|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56351164}}, but efforts by researchers and physicians led to its eradication, declared in 1996. The shift from rice cultivation to fruit farming since the 1960s was partially motivated by disease control efforts.
